title: 第 106 節
en: 3 Asau purusha/^ should probably be omitted.
4 These lines are supposed to be uttered by a man in the hour of death.

6 The Va^asaneyi-saffzhita reads : Om, krato smara, k/zb>e smara, krz'ta/w smara. Uva/a holds that Agni, fire, who has been worshipped in youth and manhood, is here invoked in the form of , mind, or that kratu is meant for sacrifice. ' Agni, remember me ! Think of the world ! Remember my deeds ! '
Keep far from us crooked evil, and we shall offer thee the fullest praise! (Rv. I, 189, 1.)

This Upanishad, though apparently simple and intelligible, is in reality one of the most difficult to understand properly. Coming at the end of the Va^asaneyi-sarahita, in which the sacrifices and the hymns to be used by the officiating priests have been described, it begins by declaring that all has to be surrendered to the Lord. The name is, lord, is peculiar, as having a far more personal colouring than Atman, Self, or Brahman, the usual names given by the Upanishads to what is the object of the highest knowledge.

Next follows a permission to continue the performance of sacrifices, provided that all desires have been surrendered. And here occurs our first difficulty, which has perplexed ancient as well as modern commentators.

I shall try, first of all, to justify my own translation. I hold that the Upanishad wishes to teach the uselessness by themselves of all good works, whether we call them sacrificial, legal, or moral, and yet, at the same time, to recognise, if not the necessity, at least the harmlessness of good works, provided they are performed without any selfish motives, without any desire of reward, but simply as a preparation for higher knowledge, as a means, in fact, of subduing all passions, and producing that serenity of mind without which man is incapable of receiving the highest knowledge. From that point of view the Upanishad may well say, Let a man wish to live here his appointed time, let him even perform all works. If only he knows that all must be surrendered to the Lord, then the work done by him will not cling to him. It will not work on and produce effect after effect, nor will it involve him in a succession of new births in which to enjoy the reward of his works, but it will leave him free to enjoy the blessings of the highest knowledge. It will have served as a preparation for that higher knowledge which the Upanishad imparts, and which secures freedom from further births.

The expression ' na karma lipyate nare ' seems to me to admit of this one explanation only, viz. that work done does not cling to man, provided he has acquired the highest knowledge. Similar expressions occur again and again. Lip was, no doubt, used originally of evil deeds which became, as it were, engrained in man ; but afterwards of all work, even of good work, if done with a desire of reward. The doctrine of the Upanishads is throughout that orthodoxy and sacrifice can procure a limited beatitude only, VAGASANEYI-SAil/IIITA-UPANISIIAD. 3 I 5 and that they are a hindrance to real salvation, which can be obtained by knowledge alone. In our passage therefore we can recognise one meaning only, viz. that work does not cling to man or stain him, if only he knows, i. e. if he has been enlightened by the Upanishad.

13; 14, takes the same view of this passage. The opponent of Badarayawa, in this case, Gsl\ ini himself, maintains that karma, work, is indispensable to knowledge, and among other arguments, he says, III, 4, 7, that it is so ' Niyamat,' ' Because it is so laid down by the law.' The passage here referred to is, according to iSahkara, our very verse, which, he thinks, should be translated as follows : ' Let a man wish to live a hundred years here (in this body) performing works ; thus will an evil deed not cling to thee, while thou art a man ; there is no other way but this by which to escape the influence of v orks.' In answer to this, Badarayawa says, first of all, III, 4, 13, that this rule may refer to all men in general, and not to one who knows; or, III, 4, 14, if it refers to a man who knows, that then t e permission to perform works is only intended to exalt the value of knowledge, the meaning being that even to a man who performs sacrifices all his life, work does not cling, if only he knows ; — such being the power of knowledge.

The same .Sahkara, however, who here sees quite clearly that this verse refers to a man who knows, explains it in the Upanishad as referring to a man who does not know (itarasyanatma^fiatayatmagrahawlraktasya). It would then mean : ' Let such a one, while performing works here on earth, wish to live a hundred years. In this manner there is no other way for him but this (the performance of sacrifices), so that an evil deed should not be engrained, or so that he should not be stained by such a deed.' The first and second ver.es of th t Upanishad would thus represent the two paths of life, that of knowledge and that of works, and the following verses would explain the rewards assigned to e:;ch.

Mahidhara, in his commentary on the Va^asaneyi-sawhita, steers at first a middle course. He would translate : ' Let one who performs the Agnihotra and other sacrifices, without any desire of reward, wish to live here a hundred years. If thou do so, there will be salvation for thee, not otherwise There are many roads that lead to heaven, but one only leading to salvation, namely, performance of good works, without any desire of reward, which produces a pure heart. Work thus done, merely as a preparation for salvation, does not cling to man, i.e. it produces a pure heart, but does not 3 I 6 VAGASANEYI-SAMHITA-UPANISHAD.

entail any further consequences.' So far he agrees with Uva/a's explanation1. He allows, however, another explanation also, so that the second line would convey the meaning : ' If a man lives thus (performing good works), then there is no other way by which an evil deed should not be engrained; i.e. in order to escape the power of sin, he must all his life perform sacred acts.'

Next follows a description of the lot of those who, immersed in works, have not arrived at the highest knowledge, and have not recovered their true self in the Highest Self, or Brahman, That Brahman, though the name is not used here, is then described, and salvation is promised to the man who beholds all things in the Self and the Self in all things.

The verses 9-14 are again full of difficulty, not so much in themselves as in their relation to the general system of thought which prevails in the Upanishads, and forms the foundation of the Vedanta philosophy. The commentators vary considerably in their interpretations. £ankara explains avidya, not-knowledge, by good works, particularly sacrifice, performed with a hope of reward; vidya, or knowledge, by a knowledge of the gods, but not, as yet, of the highest Brahman. The former is generally supposed to lead the sacrificer to the pitrzloka, the world of the fathers, from whence he returns to a series of new births ; the latter to the devaloka, the world of the gods, from whence he may either proceed to Brahman, or enter upon a new round of existences. The question then arises, how in our passage the former could be said to lead to blind darkness, the latter to still greater darkness. But for that statement, I have no doubt that all the commentators would, as usual, have taken vidya for the knowledge of the Highest Brahman, and avidya for orthodox belief in the gods and good works, the former securing immortality in the sense of freedom from new births, while the reward of the latter is blessedness in heaven for a limited period, but without freedom from new births.

This antithesis between vidya and avidya seems to me so firmly established that I cannot bring myself to surrender it here. Though this Upanishad has its own very peculiar character, yet its object is, after all, to impart a knowledge of the Highest Self, and not to inculcate merely a difference between faith in the ordinary gods and good works. It was distinctly said before (ver. 3), that those who have destroyed their self, i.e. who perform works only, Uva/a explains ^ishivishe/* for ^i^ivishet as a purushavyataya^.

VAGASANEYI-SAMHITA-UPANISHAD. 3 I 7 and have not arrived at a knowledge of the true Self, go to the worlds of the Asuras, which are covered with blind darkness. If then the same blind darkness is said in verse 9 to be the lot of those who worship not-knowledge, this can only mean those who have not discovered the true Self, but are satisfied with the performance of good works. And if those who perform good works are opposed to others who delight in true knowledge, that knowledge can be the knowledge of the true Self only.

The difficulty therefore which has perplexed Sankara is this, how, while the orthodox believer is said to enter into blind darkness, the true disciple, who has acquired a knowledge of the true Self, could be said to enter into still greater darkness. While •Sankara in this case seems hardly to have caught the drift of the Upanishad, Uva/a and Mahidhara propose an explanation which is far more satisfactory. They perceive that the chief stress must be laid on the words ubhayaw saha, ' both together,' in verses 1 1 and 14. The doctrine of certain Vedanta philosophers was that works, though they cannot by themselves lead to salvation, are useful as a preparation for the highest knowledge, and that those who imagine that they can attain the highest knowledge without such previous preparation, are utterly mistaken. From this point of view therefore the author of the Upanishad might well say that those who give themselves to what is not knowledge, i. e. to sacrificial and other good works, enter into darkness, but that those who delight altogether in knowledge, despising the previous discipline of works, deceive themselves and enter into still greater darkness.

Then follows the next verse, simply stating that, according to the teaching of wise people, the reward of knowledge is one thing, the reward of ignorance, i.e. trust in sacrifice, another. Here Mahidhara is right again by assigning the pitrzloka, the world of the fathers, as the reward of the ignorant; the devaloka, the world of the gods, as the reward of the enlightened, provided that from the world of the gods they pass on to the knowledge of the Highest Self or Brahman.

The third verse contains the strongest confirmation of Mahidhara's view. Here it is laid down distinctly that he only who knows both together, both what is called ignorance and what is called knowledge, can be saved, because by good works he overcomes death, here explained by natural works, and by knowledge he obtains the Immortal, here explained by oneness with the gods, the last step that leads on to oneness with Brahman.
Uva/a, who takes the same view of these verses, explains at once, 3 I 8 VAGASANEYI-SAMHITA-UPANISHAD.

and even more boldly than Mahidhara \ vidya, or knowledge, by brahmavujnana, knowledge of Brahman, which by itself, and if not preceded by works, leads to even greater darkness than what is called ignorance, i. e. sacrifice and orthodoxy without knowledge.

The three corresponding verses, treating of sambhuti and asambhuti instead of vidya and avidya, stand first in the Va^asaneyisawhita. They must necessarily be explained in accordance with our explanation of the former verses, i. e. sambhuti must correspond to vidya, it must be meant for the true cause, i. e. for Brahman, while asambhuti must correspond with avidya, as a name of what is not real, but phenomenal only and perishable.

Mahidhara thinks that these verses refer to the Bauddhas, which can hardly be admitted, unless we take Buddhist in a very general sense. Uva/a puts the Lokayatas in their place 2. It is curious also to observe that Mahidhara, following Uva/a, explains asambhuti at first by the denial of the resurrection of the body, while he takes sambhuti rightly for Brahman. I have chiefly followed Uva/a's commentary, except in his first explanation of asambhuti, resurrection3. In what follows Uva/a explains sambhuti rightly by the only cause of the origin of the whole world, i. e. Brahman 4, while he takes v'masa, destruction, as a name of the perishable body5.

Sahkara sees much more in these three verses than Uva/a. He takes asambhuti as a name of Prakrz'ti, the undeveloped cause, sambhuti as a name of the phenomenal Brahman or Hira;zyagarbha. From a worship of the latter a man obtains supernatural powers, from devotion to the former, absorption in Prakrz'ti.

Mahidhara also takes a similar view, and he allows, like Sahkara, another reading, viz. sambhutim av'masam fa, and avinarena mrztyum tirtva. In this case the sense would be : 'He who knows the worship both of the developed and the undeveloped, overcomes 1 Mahidhara decides in the end that vidya and amn'tam must here be taken in a limited or relative sense, tasmad vidyopasanamrz'tam /Japekshikam iti dik, and so agrees on the whole with -Sahkara, pp. 25-27.
2 Shad anush/ubha/z, lokayatika/5 prastuyante yesham etad danyanam.
8 Mrz'tasya sata/5 puna/^ sambhavo nasti, ata/^ jariragrahwad asmakam muktir eva.
4 Samastasya gagata/i sambhavaikahetu brahma. fi Vinaja^ vimui ka vapu£ jariram.

VAGASANEYI-SAMHITA-UPANISHAD. 3 I 9 death, i. e. such evil as sin, passion, &c, through worship of the undeveloped, while he obtains through worship of the developed, i. e. of Hirawyagarbha, immortality, absorption in Prakr/ti.'

All these forced explanations to which the commentators have recourse, arise from the shifting views held by various authorities with regard to the value of works. Our Upanishad seems to me to propound the doctrine that works, though in themselves useless, or even mischievous, if performed with a view to any present or future rewards, are necessary as a preparatory discipline. This is or was for a long time the orthodox view. Each man was required to pass through the ajramas, or stages of student and householder, before he was admitted to the freedom of a Sannyasin. As on a ladder, no step was to be skipped. Those who attempted to do so, were considered to have broken the old law, and in some respects they may indeed be looked upon as the true precursors of the Buddhists.
zh: 3 阿沙普魯沙（Asau purusha）應該可能被省略。
據稱，這些詩句應當由一個人在臨終時刻所誦詞。
6 《瓦亞薩涅依經集》（Vayas­aneyi-samhita）載有：Om, krato smara, kshaye smara, krita smara。烏瓦塔（Uvata）認為，在青年及壯年時期受奉祀的火神阿格尼（Agni），在此乃以心智（kratu）的形式被喚起，或者說克拉圖（kratu）是指祭祀。「阿格尼啊，憶我！思慮世界！憶我之行為！」
願你遠離我們彎曲的邪惡，我們將獻上最充分的讚頌！（梨俱吠陀 I, 189, 1.）

這部奧義書雖然表面上看來簡樸易懂，實際上卻是最難正確理解的之一。它出現在《瓦亞薩涅義-薩罕希塔》(Vaasaneyi-sarahita) 的末尾，該文獻已描述了祭祀儀式與供奉祭司所用的讚歌，而本奧義書開篇即宣告必須將一切奉獻給主。「主」(Lord) 這個名稱之所以特殊，在於它比「自我」(Atman)、「梵」(Brahman) 等名稱具有遠為鮮明的人格色彩——而「自我」與「梵」是奧義書通常用來指稱最高知識之對象的慣常名稱。
接著是允許繼續進行祭祀儀式，但須先放棄一切欲望。此處出現我們首次遇到的困難，這個問題既困擾了古代註釋家，也困擾了現代註釋家。

我首先要為自己的譯文辯護。我認為《奧義書》希望教導人們領悟一切善行本身的無用性，不論我們是稱之為祭祀的、法律的或道德的善行；然而與此同時，《奧義書》也承認——即使不是必然性，至少也是善行的無害性——只要這些善行是在沒有任何自私動機、沒有任何求報的欲望下進行的，而只是作為準備更高知識的基礎，實際上是制伏所有情慾、產生那種不具備它人就無法接受最高知識的心靈寧靜的手段。從這一觀點出發，《奧義書》理所當然可以說：「願人在此地享有其定命的壽年，願他甚至履行所有的善行。只要他知道一切都必須歸奉於主，那麼由他所行之工就不會執著於他。」它不會延續下去、產生一個效果接著一個效果，也不會使他陷入一連串新的轉生中去享受其善行的報酬，反而會使他自由地享受最高知識的福祉。它將成為《奧義書》所傳授之那更高知識的準備，而這更高知識保證了人們不再輪迴轉生的自由。

「業不著人」（na karma lipyate nare）這個表達式在我看來只能有一個解釋，即業行不黏著於人，但前提是他已獲得最高知識。類似的表達式一次又一次地出現。Lip 無疑原本是用來指邪惡的行為，這些行為彷彿會深深銘刻在人身上；但之後它被用於所有的業行，甚至是善行，只要它是出於求報酬的願望而為。《奧義書》的教義自始至終主張，正統信仰與祭祀只能獲致有限的福樂，而它們乃是真實救贖的障礙，唯有通過知識才能獲得真實救贖。因此在我們的經文中，我們只能識別出一個含義，即業行不黏著於人也不玷污他，但前提是他知曉，亦即他已被《奧義書》所啟蒙。

13；14，對這段經文持同樣看法。巴達拉亞那（Badarayawa）的反對者，在此情況下是高卡里尼（Gsl\ini）本人，主張業（karma）、行為（work）對於知識是不可或缺的，並在其他論證中，他在《梵經》III, 4, 7 處提出，之所以如此是「尼亞瑪特」（'Niyamat'），即「因為法則如此規定」（'Because it is so laid down by the law'）。此處所指的經文，根據商羯羅（Sankara）的解釋，正是我們的這一句，他認為應翻譯如下：「願人在此（在此身體中）在此生活百年，執行功業；如此惡行將不會黏著於汝，當汝還是人類之時；除此之外別無他法，可以逃脫業的影響。」對此，巴達拉亞那首先在《梵經》III, 4, 13 處回應，謂此法則可能適用於所有人一般，而非適用於知者；或在III, 4, 14處，若其確實適用於知者，則執行功業的許可只是為了凸顯知識的價值，意思是即使一個人終身執行祭祀，如果他具有知識，業也將不會黏著於他；——此乃知識的力量所在。

同樣是這位商羯羅，儘管他在此處清楚地看到這段詩文係指一個有知之人，但在《奧義書》中他卻將其解釋為指一個無知之人（itarasyanatma fiatayatmagrahawlraktasya）。據此解釋，其意義應為：「如此之人在此地執行業行時，當願活百年。如此方式對他而言別無他途，唯此而已（執行祭祀），俾其惡業不致根深蒂固，或俾其不為此等業行所玷污。」該《奧義書》之第一、二詩節由此將代表人生之二道，即知識之道與業行之道，而其後之詩節則將闡明分別配予各道之報償。
瑪希陀羅在其《瓦亞薩奈亞往世書》（Vāyasaneyi-saṁhitā）的註釋中，起初採取了折中之道。他的譯文為：「凡行火祭及其他祭祀者，若無任何報酬之欲，願在此活上百年。若汝如此行，則汝將獲得救贖，否則不然。通往天堂之路甚多，但通往救贖之路唯一，即行善業，毫無報酬之欲，此善業能淨化心靈。如此所行之業，乃為救贖之準備，不執著於人身，即產生純淨之心，但不」
不會引起任何進一步的後果。』到此為止，他同意烏瓦拉（Uvata）的解釋¹。然而，他也允許另一種解釋，使得第二行的意思為：『如果一個人這樣生活（履行善行），那麼就沒有其他的方式，使得惡行不會被深深銘刻；亦即為了逃脫罪業的力量，他必須一生都在從事聖禮行為。』
接著描述了那些沉溺於業行之中、尚未抵達最高知識、未曾在至高的自我或梵（Brahman）中恢復其真實自我之人的境遇。那梵，雖然此處未使用其名稱，隨後被加以描述，而救贖則承諾給予那能在自我中觀照萬物，並在萬物中觀照自我之人。

第9至14頌又充滿困難，不必然源於它們本身，而毋寧在於它們與普遍流行於奧義書中的思想體系之間的關係，而該體系乃是吠檀多哲學的基礎。註釋者在其解釋上差異頗大。商羯羅解釋阿毘陀雅（avidya）即無知為善業，特別是伴隨對果報之希冀而進行的祭祀；毘陀雅（vidya）即知識為對提婆之知識，但尚非至高梵天之知識。前者通常被認為引導祭祀者至彼祖世界（pitṛ-loka），即先祖之境，從彼處他將返回一系列新生；後者則導至提婆世界（deva-loka），即提婆之境，從彼處他既可前往梵天，亦可進入新的輪迴。然則問題隨之而生，即我們的經文中何以前者會被說成通向盲目黑暗，而後者通向更大的黑暗。然而對於該陳述，我毫不懷疑所有的註釋者按照慣例應當已將毘陀雅理解為至高梵天之知識，而將阿毘陀雅理解為對提婆與善業之正統信仰；前者確保不朽性，乃是指免於新生，而後者之報酬則為有限期間內天界之至福，然而仍不免於新生。
這種 vidya 與 avidya 之間的對立，在我看來是如此牢固地確立，以至於我無法在此放棄它。儘管這部奧義書具有其本身極為獨特的性質，但其目的終究是傳授關於最高自我的知識，而非僅僅灌輸對普通提婆的信仰與良善行為之間的差異。前文（第3節）已明確所述，那些摧毀了自我者，即僅執行業行者，Uva/a 解釋 ^ishivishe/* 為 ^i^ivishet（作為 purushavyataya^）。
瓦迦三涅依-三希塔-奧義書（Vagasaneyi-Samhita-Upanishad）3 I 7，而未能抵達對真實自我（Self）的認知，則當往阿修羅（Asuras）之世界，彼世界為盲目之暗所籠罩。若如第9頌所言，同樣的盲目之暗乃是那些崇拜非知識者的分際，此只能意謂那些未曾發現真實自我，卻滿足於行善功者。而若行善功者與他人相對立，後者樂於真實知識，則該知識只能是對真實自我的知識。

因此，困擾商羯羅的難題是：為何正統的信徒據說進入盲目的黑暗，而獲得真我（true Self）知識的真正門徒卻被說成進入更深的黑暗。雖然商羯羅在此案例中似乎未能完全掌握《奧義書》的要旨，但烏瓦拉（Uvata）與瑪希達拉（Mahidhara）提出的解釋卻遠為令人滿意。他們察覺到重點應著重於第11及14節中「ubhayaw saha」（雙雙一起）的措詞。某些韋丹塔哲學家的教義是：雖然業行本身不足以引致救贖，但作為通往最高知識的準備是有益的，而那些想像自己無需如此事先準備就能證得最高知識的人，實屬大謬。從此觀點言之，《奧義書》的作者當可言之成理地說，那些沉溺於非知識者，亦即祭祀及其他善業者，進入黑暗；但那些全然樂此於知識、鄙視業行之事先修習者，自欺欺人，進入更深的黑暗。
接著下一句經文，簡單陳述根據智者的教導，知識的報酬是一回事，無知的報酬，也就是對祭祀的信心，又是另一回事。在此馬希達拉（Mahidhara）的解釋再次正確，他將皮陀羅洛卡（pitrzloka）──父輩的世界──指定為無知者的報酬；將提婆洛卡（devaloka）──提婆的世界──指定為開悟者的報酬，前提是他們從提婆的世界進而獲得對至高自我或梵的知識。
第三頌包含對馬希達拉（Mahidhara）觀點最有力的確認。這裡明確規定，唯有同時認知兩者者——既認知所謂的無明，亦認知所謂的知識——才能得救，因為藉由善業他克服了死亡（此處解釋為自然之業），而藉由知識他獲得了不朽（此處解釋為與提婆的合一），此乃通往與梵天合一的最後一步。
烏瓦/亞（Uva/a）對這些經文持相同看法，立即解釋道，3 I 8《瓦迦桑尼耶-桑希塔-奧義書》（VAGASANEYI-SAMHITA-UPANISHAD）。
甚至比摩訶持者（Mahidhara）更大膽地將*vidya*（知識）定義為梵知（brahmavujnana），即對梵的認識，此知若孤立存在，且不以業行為先導，反而會導致比所謂無明——即無知識之祭祀與正統教義——更深的黑暗。

論及 sambhuti 與 asambhuti 而非 vidya 與 avidya 的三段相應的經文，在《瓦雅薩涅耶依往世書》(Vaasaneyisawhita) 中首先出現。它們必然要按照我們對前述經文的解釋而加以說明，即 sambhuti 必須對應於 vidya，它必須被理解為真實的原因，亦即為梵天（Brahman），而 asambhuti 必須對應於 avidya，作為非實有者的名稱，但僅為現象性的與易壞的事物。

馬希達拉認為這些詩句是指佛教徒，除非我們以非常寬泛的意義來理解佛教，否則這種看法難以接受。烏瓦拉把洛迦亞塔派（Lokayatas）放在他們的位置上²。有趣的是，馬希達拉跟隨烏瓦拉，起初將「非成就」（asambhuti）解釋為否定肉體的復活，而他卻正確地將「成就」（sambhuti）理解為梵天。除了烏瓦拉對「非成就」（asambhuti）的首個解釋——復活³——之外，我主要採用了烏瓦拉的註疏。在以下段落中，烏瓦拉正確地將「成就」（sambhuti）解釋為整個世界存在的唯一原因，即梵天⁴，而他則將「毀壞」（v'masa）理解為易朽肉體的一個名稱⁵。
商羯羅在這三節經文中看出的內涵遠比烏瓦拉更多。他將「非成有」（asambhuti）視為實性（Prakṛti）的名稱，即未開展的因，而將「成有」（sambhuti）視為現象界的梵或金胎（Hiraṇyagarbha）的名稱。從對後者的禮拜，人們可以獲得超自然的力量；從對前者的虔敬奉獻，則可獲得與實性的合一。

摩醫陀羅（Mahidhara）也持類似的觀點，他如同商羯羅一樣，允許另一種讀法，即 sambhutim av'masam fa 及 avinarena mrztyum tirtva。在此情況下，其義為：「凡知曉已成就與未成就之兩者崇拜者，克服」¹ 摩醫陀羅最終裁定，vidya 與 amn'tam 在此必須按有限或相對的意義來理解，tasmad vidyopasanamrz'tam /Japekshikam iti dik，因此在整體上與商羯羅的見解相符，見頁 25-27。

瓦迦桑耶吠陀本集奧義書（Vagasaneyi-Samhita-Upanishad）3，1，9 死亡，即如罪惡、情欲等此類邪惡，乃由於敬拜未開發者而得；而他由敬拜已開發者，即金胎（Hirawyagarbha）而獲得不朽、融攝於物質性（Prakr/ti）。

所有這些評釋者所採取的勉強解釋，都源於各個權威對業報（works）價值的觀點變動。我認為本奧義書提出的學說是：業報本身雖然無用，甚至在為了追求任何現世或來世報酬而執行時反而有害，但作為預備性的修行紀律卻是必要的。這曾經是並且長期以來都是正統的觀點。每個人在被認可獲得遁世者（Sannyasin）的自由之前，都必須經歷阿什拉瑪（ajramas），即學生和家主這兩個人生階段。如同攀爬梯子，任何一級都不能跳過。那些試圖這樣做的人被認為違反了古老的法則，在某些方面他們確實可以被視為佛教的真正先驅。
